summary:
Sargon of Akkad, who reigned approximately 2334–2279 BC, is widely regarded as the world's first empire builder — a ruler who united the diverse city-states of Mesopotamia under a single centralised authority for the first time in recorded history. His origins are obscure and heavily mythologised; the Sargon Legend describes him as a child of humble birth abandoned in a basket on a river, a narrative that parallels later stories about figures such as Moses. He rose to prominence as the cup-bearer of the king of Kish before seizing power and founding the city of Akkad, which gave his dynasty and language their names.

Over the course of a long and vigorous reign, Sargon systematically conquered the Sumerian city-states of lower Mesopotamia, defeating their rulers and installing Akkadian governors in their place. His campaigns extended beyond Mesopotamia itself, reportedly reaching Anatolia, the Levant, and possibly the Persian Gulf trading network. He claimed in his royal inscriptions to have washed his weapons in the sea at the edge of his realm, a poetic assertion of universal dominion. By the end of his reign the [[event:45|Akkadian Empire]] stretched from the Persian Gulf to northern Syria.

Sargon's administrative innovations were as significant as his military conquests. He replaced local rulers with appointed governors, standardised weights and measures, promoted the Akkadian language alongside Sumerian in official documents, and established a professional standing army — a crucial innovation in an era when armies had typically been mustered seasonally from agricultural labour. He also appointed his daughter [[event:1729|Enheduanna]] as High Priestess of the moon god Nanna at Ur, a strategic religious appointment that helped consolidate Akkadian authority over Sumerian sacred institutions.

Sargon's legacy was immense. He became a legendary figure throughout ancient Mesopotamia, and later Assyrian and Babylonian kings invoked his name and modelled themselves on his image. The name Sargon was adopted by at least two Assyrian monarchs centuries later. His empire, while relatively short-lived in comparison to later imperial projects, established a template for universal rule in Mesopotamia — the idea that one king could and should govern all of the known world — that shaped political thought in the region for two thousand years.

The city of Akkad itself has never been definitively located by archaeologists, making Sargon's capital one of the great lost cities of antiquity. The collapse of [[event:45|the Akkadian Empire]] after [[event:1723|Naram-Sin's reign]] and the subsequent Gutian invasions brought an end to his dynasty, but the cultural synthesis of Sumerian and Akkadian that his empire facilitated became the shared inheritance of all subsequent Mesopotamian civilisations.
